title: Penn State baseball's Michael Anderson named First Team All-Big Ten
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Designated hitter Michael Anderson was named to the First Team All-Big Ten list following his record-breaking 2026 season.

𝑭𝒊𝒓𝒔𝒕 𝑻𝒆𝒂𝒎 𝑨𝒍𝒍-𝑩𝒊𝒈 𝑻𝒆𝒏After one of the best seasons in school history, @Michael_A110 earned All-Big Ten honors!!!#WeAre pic.twitter.com/fl7OvmPiSk— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) May 19, 2026

Anderson made an immediate impact after transferring from Arkansas, starting in 50 of the Nittany Lions' 51 games this year. He notched a .358 batting average and a program record 20 home runs, totaling 53 RBIs.

The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native was another product of coach Mike Gambino's push to bring top talent back to their home state. Last season, Roaring Springs, Pennsylvania, native Paxton Kling was named to the First Team All-Big Ten team, as well, marking the first time since 2011 and 2012 that the blue and white have players on the first team in back-to-back years.

Anderson will now turn to the 2026 MLB Draft, with the opportunity to get drafted and turn pro in July.

title: Film review | 4-star RB Aiden Gibson possesses power running, receiving
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State earned a commitment from 4-star running back Aiden Gibson last week, who became the first of two running backs to pledge during the week. Gibson was joined by 3-star Chukwuma Odoh a few days later, giving the Nittany Lions a strong 2027 running back duo.

Gibson committed to Penn State as the No. 322 player in the class, No. 22 running back and No. 8 player in South Carolina. He’s added onto a recruiting class that has sought out talent from all over the country, with Gibson becoming the first player out of South Carolina in the class.

Gibson was a major get for the Nittany Lions, possessing strong talent from the running back position. Mixed with Odoh’s quickness comes Gibson’s thunder, potentially bringing that thunder and lightning duo back that Penn State had with Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

Here’s a deeper look at some things that stand out in Gibson's film.

Downhill running

While Gibson doesn’t have the home run speed that a runner like Odoh has, he still hits the hole hard and has a downhill running style.

Gibson showcased an ability to break off big runs with his capability to make quick decisions, helping find open gaps and get downhill quickly.

Gibson has a downhill running style, similar to that of former Nittany Lion Kaytron Allen, finding gaps quicklyHere’s him finding the gap and turning an outside run into a big gain: pic.twitter.com/RdZDWZn5aG— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Penn State has had success with this style of runner, as Allen had a record-breaking senior year and has the most career rushing yards in program history. While Gibson likely won’t see the field as much as Allen did in his first season, the running styles are pretty similar.

Gibson doesn’t have home run speed, but he’s fast enough to have big runs and get in the end zone from far out pic.twitter.com/DY0Nkh6Hoe— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson’s downhill running allowed him to rush for over seven yards per carry in 2025, eclipsing 1,500 yards for a second consecutive season.

Tough to tackle

In addition to Gibson’s downhill running style comes his ability to break tackles. Across his tape, he often fights off tackles to extend run plays even longer.

Gibson has a strong ability to make defenders miss, using his balance and strength to fend off tackles pic.twitter.com/FYgFagAeUO— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson’s 6-foot-1, 212-pound frame could prove to be more college ready, allowing him to progress quicker in the offseason. His frame has made it easier to fend off tackles at the high school level, and with his powerful running he could see early playing time.

Not only is he strong and has firm balance, he has an underrated ability to make defenders miss with juke moves. The combination allows him to be a dynamic running back who can beat defenses in many different ways.

It’s not just his strength and balance that allows him to break tackles. He also has quick feet allowing him to juke past quicker cornerbacks pic.twitter.com/YRvhs1RJss— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

The South Carolina native’s power is next level, as he can run through tackles in addition to fending off defenders. This combo of power and strength with his downhill running makes for a very intriguing prospect and a name that could add to the recent success of Penn State running backs.

Receiving threat

It’s not just in the run game that Gibson makes an impact — he’s shown signs of being a reliable receiver in the pass game, whether it’s out of the backfield or lined up out wide.

Gibson shows a strong ability to catch the ball out of the backfield. Whether it’s screens or short routes, his hands allow him to quickly asses upfield to turn small plays into bigger ones pic.twitter.com/rbk0DsNPfv— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

In what will be a much more versatile offense at Penn State, Gibson’s ability to catch the ball will be vital for his future in the program. Gibson showed a plethora of routes, with catches in the backfield and an ability to win one-on-one down the field.

Last season, Gibson had 387 receiving yards and four receiving touchdowns, proving to be a versatile threat. While the offense he’ll be in at Penn State didn’t utilize the running backs in the pass game too much, it also didn’t have the receiving threat that a running back like Gibson provides.

His versatility showcases his ability to also be a threat out wide as an extra receiver pic.twitter.com/Kwb2lobm6A— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson proves he can be a difference maker time and time again, whether it’s getting handoffs or catching the ball. No matter how he’s utilized in the offense, he has the ability to make big plays and be a drive-changer at any given moment.

title: Former Penn State commit Layton von Brandt commits to Auburn
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State commit has decided on his collegaite home.

Layton von Brandt has commitmented to Auburn, he announced via X on Monday.

The offensive tackle comitted to Penn State in 2024, but decommitted after James Franklin was fired in October 2025 along with the rest of the Nittany Lions' 2027 class at the time.

Von Brandt is the No. 5 offensive tackle in his class, according to 247Sports composite. The 4-star is also the No. 64 player in his class and best athlete out of Delaware.

The Delaware native had the Nittany Lions, Florida, Notre Dame and Auburn in his final four schools, ultimately deciding on Alex Golesh and the Tigers.

Von Brandt still has official visits scheduled with each school in his final four, and he'll be in Happy Valley on June 2.

title: ‘He had an incredible game’ | Preston Hawkins posts career day despite tournament loss to No. 8 Princeton
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — As the sun beat down on the artificial turf at Delaware Stadium, on-field temperatures reached 112 degrees as No. 8 Penn State fell to No. 1 Princeton in the NCAA quarterfinal.

The FieldTurf surface emblazoned with a grinning Blue Hen wasn't the hottest thing in Newark on Sunday afternoon, however, as the dueling goaltenders stole the show in the Tigers’ 14-10 win.

“It was pretty brutal,” Princeton midfielder Jackson Green said. “These are big games, so you’ve just got to fight through it. The weather’s the same for both teams.”

Freshman netminder Preston Hawkins made his presence felt immediately, stuffing a shot from Colin Burns. The save sparked a transition opportunity for the blue and white, one that defenseman Alex Ross capitalized on with a tone-setting opening goal.

Hawkins notched six saves in the first quarter, and nine total in the first half, keeping the matchup knotted at 6-6 after a half hour of play.

“He had an incredible game,” Princeton midfielder Tucker Wade said. “It felt like throughout the game we were getting good looks, and just couldn’t get past him for a while.”

Eventually, Wade and the rest of the Tigers were able to find the nylon, scoring eight times in the second half. The junior out of Bethesda, Maryland, was most successful, scoring four times in the victory over the Nittany Lions.

Despite the loss, Hawkins made eight stops in the final two frames to set a new career-high of 17 saves, besting his former record of 14 that came in Penn State’s March 21 victory over Maryland in Happy Valley.

After the win against the Terrapins, the Powell, Ohio, native was ecstatic as he addressed the media. Two months later, Hawkins was in tears as he sat between teammate Chase Robertson and coach Jeff Tambroni inside the Bob Carpenter Center adjacent to the field where he’d just concluded his debut campaign as a Nittany Lion.

“Princeton’s a good team, they have a lot of good shooters,” Hawkins said. “The defense was in front of me, giving me the shots I wanted to see. They were the reason I was successful.”

While he buried his third career goal to begin the day, Ross anchored that unit in what was his final collegiate appearance. Hawkins credited the senior not only for his performance on Sunday, but for his leadership all season.

“I’ve looked up to him all year,” Hawkins said. “I wouldn’t be here without him. He’s given me so much guidance. He’s made me feel comfortable on the field.”

While he plays on the opposite end of the field, Robertson echoed his goaltender’s thoughts on the PLL-bound Ross.

“I’m just so thankful to have him as a teammate and as a friend,” Robertson said. “Every game he was always motivating us. He took me under his wing, and as a sophomore he helped me improve my game. He’s going to do great things in the future.”

80 yards away from Hawkins, Ryan Croddick delivered a juxtaposing but equally as important performance for Princeton. Croddick, the reigning Ivy League Goaltender of the Year, made eight saves in his squad’s win, but just two in the first half.

“Penn State has such an incredibly disciplined offense,” Princeton coach Matt Madalon said. “I don’t think they settled for a shot. With (Hunter) Aquino off the field, they’re not taking a shot outside of eight yards, so it’s not a game where a goalie is going to get a ton of rhythm.”

Six saves from the senior in the final two quarters saw the blue and white score just four goals, and were instrumental in ending the Nittany Lions’ season.

“We debated going into a zone to see if we could spark him with an outside save and get some transition,” Madalon said. “Luckily they settled, and he got into the game down the stretch, but (it was a) really hard game to play goalie in.”

While Croddick got his 14th victory on the season, Hawkins notched the sixth loss of his freshman year. The goaltender, alongside attackman Jack Iannantuono and midfielder Andrew Beard, played a key role in a trio of first-year stars that made an impact early in their time at Penn State.

In a season that had peaks and valleys, the brotherhood of the squad as a whole was something that the young stars could lean on, especially with a well-balanced group of seniors that have donned the blue and white for the last time.

“It hurt a lot to see my teammates cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” Robertson said. “I’m so thankful to have them as my brothers and best friends, and in my life (both) today and in the future.”

Despite a graduating class that left their impact on the program, Penn State is undoubtedly a young squad that will return the majority of their 2026 production. Tambroni, still searching for his first national championship, noted that his goal is to win while creating a lasting trend of high-level play.

“Every year, the mission is the same, to build and sustain a championship culture,” Tambroni said. “That’s where I’m most proud. I don’t think they failed today, I just think we lost today.”

title: ‘A wonderful group of young men’ | Senior class leaves loving culture despite loss in NCAA quarterfinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — Penn State’s senior class has played in four-consecutive NCAA tournaments. They’ve gotten knocked out in the first round, went to the national semifinals at Gillette Stadium and Lincoln Financial Field, but never have gotten over the hump with a title-game berth.

The 2026 season was supposed to be different. The Nittany Lions returned a senior class that featured one of the best defensemen in the nation, Alex Ross, as well as key contributors like Will Costin, Jon King and one of the deepest midfield cores in college lacrosse.

Despite falling 14-10 to No. 1 Princeton in the national quarterfinals on Sunday, the senior class left it all out there on the field and instilled a winning culture that the Nittany Lion youth will take forward in seasons to come.

“It hurts a lot to see my teammates, my brothers cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” sophomore Chase Robertson said. “They helped lead all the way down to our freshmen. I'm so thankful to have every single one of them, and it hurts to have our season go out this way.”

Robertson and freshman goaltender Preston Hawkins took to the podium following the loss with choked words. They noted that the senior class was instrumental to the team’s successes, on and off the field, fostering a welcoming environment that made the younger teammates feel at home.

“Despite the loss, there's just so much love in the locker room that you got 48 guys, coach included, just going up to each other, hugging each other, could tell each other anything,” Hawkins said. “This group's so tight, and everyone's there for everyone, so I mean, just the amount of love, it just never fades, regardless.”

Coach Jeff Tambroni, in his 26th season as a Division I head coach, has seen his fair share of senior leadership groups. Tambroni claimed that “this is one of the most balanced senior classes I've ever coached.”

“Now, statistically, it doesn't pop off the page,” Tambroni said. “You don't see a ton of goals, but you do in terms of the roles that they adopted, the confidence that they instilled, the sacrifices that they made.”

With their collegiate careers all said and done, the players will go off in different directions. Ross is headed to the New York Atlas of the Premier Lacrosse League, who Tambroni said will “likely have a long and successful PLL career.”

Others, however, may not continue at the professional level and will look back at their time at Panzer Stadium in Happy Valley. Tambroni’s way of thinking says that success isn’t built on the numbers or how many national championships are won, but if the seniors wish they did anything differently.

“My measure is when they stand up at the end of the year and they leave and we start talking about next year's team; can that group say ‘we left this program without any regrets?’” Tambroni said. “I hope that someday they'll all step back and say it was a phenomenal experience with a wonderful group of young men.”

title: Penn State track and field’s Allon Clay wins 2nd straight Big Ten outdoor title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After taking home gold at last year’s Big Ten championship, graduate student Allon Clay has done it again.

Clay won the men’s 800-meter finals at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships on Sunday, running 1:46.58.

Clay was in the lead after a 52.01-second first lap and continued to keep pace through the last 400-meters, running a 54.57 last lap to win the race.

The Fujisawa, Kanagawa, Japan, native had a stellar showing in the preliminary round on Saturday, running a meet record 1:45.11 to set the No. 3 mark in school history.

Clay is coming off an indoor season where he fell short in both the 800m and 600-meter finals. While finishing first and second in the prelims, he went on to finish second and fourth in the finals, respectively, before finishing third at the NCAA Championships.

Clay is the only Nittany Lion to have won the men’s 800m in nearly 10 years, having won the event the past two seasons.

title: Penn State track and field’s Hayley Kitching wins 4th Big Ten 800m title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Like clockwork, Penn State’s Hayley Kitching has come out on top again.

Kitching won her fourth 800-meter Big Ten title during her collegiate career, running 2:01.77 at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships.

Kitching came through the first lap in the lead, running a 59.29-second lap. Things stayed the same through the final lap, running 1:02.48 for the win.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native finished second in the 800m prelims on Saturday, running a 2:01.61. Kitching is coming off a Big Ten championship-winning indoor season, where she broke her former school record in 1:59.22.

The Nittany Lions have turned into an 800m powerhouse in recent years, as evident by Kitching’s four conference titles. Of the five women’s 800m Big Ten titles attributed to Penn State, Kitching is responsible for two of them.

Kitching entered the conference championship weekend with the 22nd-best mark in the East region and will look to carry her success to the NCAA East First Round later this month.

title: Film review | 3-star QB commit Will Wood brings strong arm, poise to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured a quarterback commitment in the Class of 2027 when Will Wood — a 3-star from Westwood, Massachusetts — announced his pledge on Friday afternoon.

Wood ranks as the No. 3 player out of Massachusetts and No. 29 quarterback in his class, per 247Sports composite. He finished up his junior year at Xaverian Brothers High School with the program’s third-consecutive MIAA Division I State Championship after throwing for over 2,800 yards and 42 touchdowns with one interception.

So far in his two years as the starter, Wood has won a pair of state championships and earned a 21-6 overall record.

Wood’s tape offers much to look at, especially with his strong arm and ability to improvise when things get messy in the pocket. His arm, crafty play and mental strength will be crucial to his growth into his role as Penn State’s potential quarterback of the future.

Here’s a more in-depth look at some of his film.

Stellar deep ball, accuracy

One of Wood’s deadliest weapons is his deep ball. It’s accurate and hits receivers in stride, specifically primary wideout Christian McIntyre, who Wood has built trust with during his time with the Hawks.

From deeper in his own end, Wood can air out passes up the middle on dig or post routes for big gains between the hashmarks. Additionally, he does well with corner routes to give his receivers space up the sidelines away from the majority of defenders.

One of Wood’s best aspects is his arm. He’s got an accurate deep ball and hits his receivers in stride on post and corner routes for big gains in the midfield and up the sidelines.Here’s a few clips of Wood showcasing his throwing power and placement accuracy: pic.twitter.com/3NiMM6wNlP— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

In the red zone, Wood often elected to attack the back of the end zone with higher passes that his receivers had to leap for.

In the endzone, Wood continues to aim long, often attacking the back of the endzone with high passes.He’s put a lot of points on the board by using every inch of the field. pic.twitter.com/OSp6VX0HGG— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

Additionally, Wood has proficiency throwing into coverage. Whenever his receivers or tight end couldn’t completely separate from the defense, Wood threaded the needle into tight spaces in a way that kept his teammates’ running momentum going, often resulting in additional yardage after the reception.

Dual-threat capabilities

Wood’s skill as a passer doesn’t end in the pocket, however. He’s efficient when scrambling out of the pocket and throwing on the run while maintaining sufficient power.

Wood isn’t just a pocket passer, however. He’s equally as strong throwing on the run when the pocket collapses.He keeps a cool head, calmly surveys and makes the best of imperfect situations. pic.twitter.com/0W6vxh9rWd— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

He’s slippery when maneuvering out of the pocket and can evade tackles to keep the play alive. On play-action, Wood is patient in getting himself and his intended receiver free before making the throw, something he can do while running on both sides of the field.

Wood’s pocket awareness allows him to get out of danger before he’s even touched, lending to his crisp uncontested throws.

When he doesn’t have an option downfield and has to run it himself, he’s quick and agile on his feet. He has a 4.67 second 40-yard dash time, and with a background as a track and field athlete, Wood is willing to make deeper runs when he feels he can gain a few extra yards after initial contact rather than opting to slide.

When there aren’t any open receivers, however, Wood isn’t shy about making a break for it himself.He has the long speed to make deep runs down the field into open space. Wood’s grit affords him a few more yards in exchange for taking a shot. pic.twitter.com/WOoM9yYUZH— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

His ability to create additional options for himself when standard pocket progression falls short will make him a threatening quarterback for collegiate defenses to work against.

Mental strength, poise

Wood is known for his calm demeanor and not folding under pressure, which is a big reason why he’s such a decorated high school athlete.

That winning culture Wood’s been surrounded by since his freshman year has fostered confidence in himself to make the big plays when they matter. It's evident in his game by the way he doesn’t back down on an opposing blitz.

When the pocket collapses, Wood doesn’t panic. He scans the field, goes through his progressions and typically makes a well-thought out pass or move to the outside before the rushers can get to him.

Wood is a difficult quarterback to sack due to his pocket awareness and ability to get a step ahead on shedding tackles.He recognizes dangerous situations a beat before they occur and steps up in the pocket for an extra split second to throw. pic.twitter.com/vkzWcdLXaX— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

If he’s forced out of the pocket, Wood always has his eyes looking upfield and more often than not will locate an open target in the nick of time.

All Wood knows is victory, which is clear considering his back-to-back state championship victories as the starter. Arguably one of his most impressive performances came in the 2025 MIAA Division I Super Bowl, where he went 11-16 for 345 yards and five touchdowns.

Wood’s calmness can prove to be his greatest strength as he makes the jump from high school to college, so long as he remains coachable and makes the adjustments he needs to perform well in a Power Four conference like the Big Ten.

title: No. 8 Penn State falls to No. 1 Princeton in NCAA quarterfinal
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — On Sunday, No. 8 Penn State and No. 1 Princeton met for the second time in the 2026 season, but the rematch at Delaware Stadium was on a much larger stage in the NCAA quarterfinals.

Despite the earlier meeting between the budding rivals ending in Penn State’s favor, the Tigers came out on top with an 14-10 victory to send them through to the national semifinals. The Nittany Lions ended their turbulent season with a 10-6 overall record and Big Ten Tournament championship.

“Congratulations to Coach Madalon, his staff, and his team, they played terrific today, and just a shout out to Penn State’s fans,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “Just really appreciative of the way they showed up today, and Delaware did a great job of hosting. I’m sorry it didn’t work out for Penn State today, but with that the whole team played really hard and gave great effort.”

First half

While Penn State got off on the right foot, the difference was that the Tigers’ shots made it on goal while the Nittany Lions often missed the net. Hawkins was forced to make the majority of the stops himself, finishing the game with a career-high 17 saves, nine of which came in the first half.

By comparison, Ryan Croddick made eight saves in the victory.

“Penn State (had) such an incredibly disciplined offense,” Princeton coach Matt Madalon said. “I don't think they settled for a shot. With Aquino off the field, they're not taking a shot outside of eight yards, so it's not a game where the goalie is going to get a ton of rhythm.”

The freshman goaltender quickly made his first stop of the game on Colin Burns. On the ensuing Penn State possession, Kyle Lehman gave the ball to Alex Ross in transition and the No. 8 overall pick in the PLL draft found the back of the cage to open the scoring.

Lehman continued to work on and off the ball, spinning the ball around the box and filling an open spot to shoot sidearmed past goaltender Ryan Croddick.

The Tigers proceeded to generate three consecutive golden opportunities to put themselves on the board. However, all three shots struck the iron — both posts and the crossbar — on the same possession.

Jake Vana broke the curse on the next shot, beating his defender 1-on-1 and threading a shot in the bottom right corner under Hawkins’ stick.

Hawkins made good on his next two chances with a high stick save on Nate Kabiri, later making a series of chest saves to keep Penn State in the lead by a goal at the end of the first quarter.

Chase Robertson drove down the middle of the box, splitting defenders and struck the back of the net to regain the two-goal lead with 13:10 remaining in the second quarter. Tucker Wade responded with a shot through traffic to bring the Tigers back within one.

Jackson Green picked off a Penn State pass in the defensive end and brought it up the field in transition. Chad Palumbo finished the job on the doorstep to tie the game, 3-3. John Jude Considine answered with a solo effort through four different Princeton defenders to dunk the ball just past Croddick, taking back the lead with 9:23 remaining in the half.

The game was tied once more when Wade stepped down into a shot that evaded Hawkins. After a media timeout, Burns jumped to snag a high pass from Palumbo and shot five-hole on Hawkins to take, giving Princeton its first lead of the game.

Parker Reynolds got his hands free by spinning away from a double team, Patrick Keenan’s slide coming flatfooted, and laced a shot past Hawkins to take a 6-4 lead.

Robertson and Andrew Beard surged with two-straight tallies late in the second quarter to tie the game 6-6 going into halftime.

Second half

The start of the second half closely mirrored the first. Hawkins immediately made a sprawling save on Nate Kabiri from his knees to start the third quarter strong. Then, Lehman rolled back off a check and fired a shot past Croddick to take a 7-6 lead.

“It was still tough, Princeton’s a really good team and they have a lot of good shooters,” Hawkins said. “But ultimately the defense was in front of me all day, giving me the shots I wanted to see. So they were the reason I was successful today.”

With 11:42 left in the quarter, Mark Watters caught Iannatuono's pass from X and slipped a low shot past Croddick short-side. Then, Porter Malkiel fell outside of the crease on a shot that beat Hawkins for the answer.

Wade secured a hat trick on the day to tie up the game, 8-8, with a bouncing shot under Hawkins’ stick with 9:13 remaining.

After a roughly seven-minute scoring drought, Palumbo scored his second goal of the game to take a 9-8 lead heading into a media timeout.

Liam Matthews tied the game, 9-9, with 45.1 seconds left in the third quarter. He was tripped and slashed on the play, the latter resulting in a minute-long man-up, and fell into a slow bouncing shot that Croddick didn’t see.

Robertson scored 58 seconds into the fourth quarter on a step-down shot that bounced past Croddick.

From there until the end, it was all Princeton.

Green stole yet another pass out of thin air and sparked a transition play to get the ball in Quinn Krammer’s stick for a game-tying goal. Princeton took the lead when John Dunphey let loose an uncontested shot after Palumbo caught a high shot behind the net for the pass, catching the Nittany Lion defense off guard.

Andrew McMeekin scored off the subsequent faceoff, blazing down the middle to give the Tigers a 12-10 lead with just over six minutes remaining in the game. Wade scored the fourth unanswered goal a minute later, forcing Tambroni to call a timeout.

The Tigers iced the game with Cooper Mueller’s goal, evading Penn State’s 10-man ride while Croddick came up big in the end.

“It hurts a lot to see my teammates, my brothers cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” Robertson said. “They helped lead all the way down to our freshmen. I'm so thankful to have every single one of them, and it hurts to have our season go out this way.”

Injury report

Patrick Carragher was listed out with an undisclosed injury that occurred in the Big Ten semifinals against Maryland. Keenan was injured in the third quarter and was out for the rest of the game.

Another race, another school record by star sophomore Ajani Dwyer.

title: Penn State track and field competes at Big Ten Championships
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three months of competition, Penn State suited up for the postseason this weekend.

The Nittany Lions competed at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championship from Friday through Sunday, earning 17 podium finishes, an eighth-place finish for the men’s team and an 11th-place finish for the women’s team.

Here’s a look at how the blue and white fared this weekend.

Friday

A trio of Nittany Lions took the field for the women’s hammer throw to kick off the event on Friday.

Senior Alex Pancoast led the way for Penn State, throwing 56.16 meters for 13th place. While Gabi Deglau threw a season best 55.32 meters, she finished just behind in 15th place.

Freshman Avery Miller competed in the first four events of the women’s heptathlon in her first-ever collegiate meet.

Miller put herself near the back of the pack following the 100-meter hurdles, where she ran 14.79 seconds. However, she continued to improve with a ninth-place finish in the high jump for 1.59 meters, a fourth-place finish in shot put for 11.71 meters and a fifth-place finish in the 200-meters with a time of 24.75 seconds. Heading into Saturday, she was in eighth place with 3,146 points.

While graduate student Collin Burkhart didn’t earn a podium finish in his final Big Ten Championship, he improved from last year’s performance, throwing 65.68 meters for seventh place.

In the women’s javelin throw, senior Audrey Friedman recorded her second-best mark of the season, finishing 14th with a throw of 47.20 meters.

In the first individual track event of the competition, senior Matt DeMatteo tied his personal best in the men’s 400-meter hurdles, running 52.17 seconds for 13th overall.

Senior Marie-Eloise Leclair booked her first finals appearance of the weekend in the women’s 200m, running 22.86 seconds wind-aided for seventh overall. She’ll compete in the finals at 3:26 p.m. on Sunday.

The biggest moment of the day for the Nittany Lions came in the men’s javelin throw, where freshman Nolan Carey propelled himself to No. 2 in school history with a seven-meter personal best. Carey threw 74.72 meters to finish fourth overall.

In the men’s 1,500-meters, junior Nick Sloff finished second in his heat and second overall, running 3:43.49 to qualify for Sunday’s finals.

Senior Claire Daniels wrapped up the action on the track Friday night, running 35:34.63 in the women’s 10,000-meters to finish 13th overall.

Saturday

Miller continued her first-ever collegiate heptathlon on Saturday morning, competing in the long jump. The Cumberland, Maryland, native jumped 5.23 meters for ninth place, dropping to ninth overall. However, she came back in the javelin throw with a mark of 38.90 meters for fourth place, moving to sixth overall with 4,415 points.

In the women’s shot put, freshman Skye-Petrie Cameron finished 10th overall with a throw of 16.22 meters. Sophomore Sasha Garnett finished just behind with 15.80 meters for 15th place.

In the women’s high jump, sophomore Hannah Riolo earned the first points for the women’s squad. Riolo tied her personal best of 1.80 meters, finishing in sixth place.

After clinching the 200m finals on Friday, Leclair earned her second finals berth in the 100m, running 11.39 seconds for fifth overall.

In the men’s 100m prelims, sophomore Ajani Dwyer broke the school record with a time of 10.04 seconds, also earning a finals berth as he won the preliminary race.

Miller capped off her first collegiate heptathlon with a first-place finish in the 800-meters, running 2:14.35 to finish with 5,317 points and fifth place. Miller recorded the eighth-best heptathlon in school history.

Penn State continued its success in the 800m as senior Hayley Kitching earned a season-best 2:01.61 to finish second overall and book a spot in the finals.

Graduate student Allison Johnson set the No. 5 mark in school history to finish ninth and earn the final spot in the finals, running 2:03.35, while freshman Boh Ritchie missed out on finals with a time of 2:05.74, finishing 15th overall.

The men’s 800m saw three qualifiers for the Nittany Lions, as graduate student Allon Clay took the top spot with a meet record and No. 3 mark in school history of 1:45.11. Junior Andrew Regnier set the No. 8 mark in school history in 1:46.10 for third place, while Niko Schultz ran 1:46.42 for sixth place.

Penn State wrapped up Saturday’s action in the 3,000-meter steeplechase, where senior Allison Dougherty finished seventh overall with a season-best 10:16.61. In the men’s race, senior Ryan Watson finished fourth in 8:54.19, while Logan Strawser finished eighth in 8:58.77, earning a combined six points.

Saturday

In just his freshman year, Trip Campbell burst onto the scene for the men’s high jump, tying for third place with a mark of 2.12 meters. Robert Allen also finished top-10, jumping 2.04 meters for ninth place.

In the men’s pole vault, senior Tristan McGarrah finished 12th overall with a mark of 5.07 meters.

After finishing second in the 1,500m prelims on Friday, Sloth came back and finished fifth overall in the finals, running a time of 4:02.42 for four points.

In the men’s shot put, graduate student Dylan Lambrecht led the way for Penn State with a top mark of 18.60 meters, finishing in ninth place. Freshman Bryce Thompson finished in 11th place with 18.26 meters, while senior Ryan Henry finished 13th with 18.00 meters.

Leclair took the track for her first of two finals appearances on Sunday, running 11.12 seconds for fifth place in the 100m. On the men’s side, Dwyer also finished fifth, running 9.93 seconds.

Penn State’s first Big Ten champion came in the women’s 800m, as Kitching claimed her second Big Ten outdoor title and fourth overall Big Ten title. The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native ran 2:01.77, followed by Johnson in ninth place in 2:09.90.

The success carried over into the men’s 800m finals, as Clay earned his second straight outdoor 800m title.

Clay ran a mark of 1:46.58 to take the top spot, followed by Schultz’ 1:47.70 for third place and Regnier’s 1:48.72 for eighth place.

Leclair finished her first and only Big Ten championship in the 200m finals, where she finished seventh overall in 22.80 seconds, earning another two points for the Nittany Lions.

The Nittany Lions wrapped up the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships in the 4x400m relay.

The women’s team went first, finishing 11th in 3:44.81 with Alexandria North, Abasiono Akpan, Ritchie and Kitching, while the men’s team finished sixth in 3:09.73 with Carter Fitzgerald, Matt DeMatteo, Schultz and Jake Palermo.

Up next

Penn State will compete in the NCAA East First Round, kicking off on Wednesday, May 27 in Lexington, Kentucky. The top 48 athletes in the region for each event will compete for a spot in the NCAA Championships.

title: Former Penn State student charged for unauthorized access of UPPS computer systems, bail set at $2M
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State student has been charged with unlawful use of a computer and computer trespass to alter data, according to a criminal complaint filed on Thursday.

According to an affidavit of probable cause, Zuoyu Wu, a former University Police and Public Safety (UPPS) student auxiliary officer, was reported to be involved in suspicious activity involving public safety communications and radio programming. These reports caused UPPS to launch a criminal investigation into Wu in July 2025.

The affidavit said Wu had expressed interest in UPPS’s Motorola radio systems and requested to be allowed to assist in programming these radios, which the affidavit said he was observed frequently doing, due to having been employed by Motorola.

Wu also claimed to have obtained a “Motorola engineer” certification. The affidavit said no documents have been produced to confirm Wu’s claims to having this certification.

Wu was witnessed to have been accessing computer programs within UPPS’s dispatch officer, which were said to contain personal identifiable information, vehicle registration data and criminal histories, according to the affidavit. Access to these programs is granted only to authorized personnel and not auxiliary officers.

Wu’s preliminary hearing is scheduled for May 20 at 8:30 a.m. He is currently incarcerated at Centre County Correctional Facility and District Judge Steven F. Lachamn set bail at $2 million.

title: Preview | No. 8 Penn State men’s lacrosse to battle No. 1 Princeton in NCAA Quarterfinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 8 Penn State made it to its second-straight NCAA Quarterfinals with a 10-6 victory over Army at Panzer Stadium.

However, the Nittany Lions are going from the frying pan into the fryer with No. 1 Princeton next on the schedule. The teams are set to face off at noon on Sunday at Delaware Stadium in Newark, Delaware.

Tale of the tape

No. 8 Penn State: (10-5, 3-2 Big Ten)

Big Ten Tournament Champions

No. 1 Princeton: (14-2, 5-1 Ivy League)

Ivy League Tournament Champions

Implications of the first meeting

Contrary to the rest of the Tigers’ season, their opening game against Penn State couldn’t have gone worse. Ranked third in the USILA preseason poll, and with the Nittany Lions coming off an overtime loss to unranked Villanova inside Holuba Hall, Princeton’s first game of the season being at Class of 1952 Stadium was supposed to be a strong start to the season.

Penn State came out of the gates with a bad taste in its mouth and put seven goals on the board in the first quarter, outpacing the Tigers en route to a 13-7 win.

The blue and white didn’t capitalize on the victory’s momentum, falling to unranked Navy, 12-11, the following week. Despite a rocky season compared to what many believed to be Penn State’s year to dominate, the Nittany Lions beat Maryland and Johns Hopkins to take home their first Big Ten tournament title since 2019.

After falling to the Nittany Lions, Princeton went on a heater until March 21, when the Tigers took their second and final loss of the regular season to the defending national champions, Cornell. However, Princeton closed out the regular season on a six-game winning streak before winning both conference tournament games to win the Ivy League title.

Sunday’s meeting will be a battle between champions of highly-regarded conferences, and while Penn State previously beat Princeton, the Tigers were only a fraction of what they’d become throughout the gauntlet that is the Ivy League schedule.

Last year’s quarterfinals

Penn State played arguably its best lacrosse of the 2025 season in the second half against the two-time defending national champions, Notre Dame, in the Annapolis, Maryland, quarterfinals.

After suffering a six-goal deficit later in the third quarter, the Nittany Lions — led by the New York Atlas’ Matt Traynor — completed a monumental comeback that sent the blue and white through to Gillette Stadium.

Princeton faced nearly the exact same scenario when it faced Syracuse in Hempstead, New York. The Tigers found themselves down 16-11 with less than a minute remaining in the third quarter, and rallied to tie the game 16-16 with half of the fourth frame to play.

Although Princeton led by a goal with less than five minutes left in the game, the Orange scored two unanswered to advance.

Penn State’s core, while having lost Traynor, Ethan Long, Kevin Parnham and Jack Fracyon, largely remains the game. Hunter Aquino, Kyle Lehman, Liam Matthews and Alex Ross were each crucial to the Nittany Lions surmounting the Fighting Irish.

Since they’re back for more quarterfinal action, that experience will be put to the test to see if it can be repeated.

Princeton, on the other hand, isn’t likely to make the same mistakes twice. Vengeance is on the Tigers’ side; the vengeance from the season opener and for what befell them in the quarterfinals last year.

Players to watch

Aquino, when he’s been healthy, has been coach Jeff Tambroni’s ace at attack. Despite missing five games due to an undisclosed injury, the sophomore paces the Nittany Lions with 26 goals. He and Lehman are currently tied for second with 35 points on the season, sitting only a single point behind Big Ten Freshman of the Year Jack Iannantuono.

Ross and Jon King are the primary threats on defense, both having been named All-Americans by Inside Lacrosse. Ross, a first-team selection, accumulated 21 caused turnovers so far this year and added 31 ground balls. King, named to the second-team, has caused 17 turnovers and scooped 36 ground balls.

Freshman Preston Hawkins tends the pipes for Penn State as a true freshman and had a standout, 12 save performance against Army in his first collegiate national tournament game. He sports a .518 save percentage and 8.53 goals-against average.

Princeton has a pair of offensive weapons at attack, both named all-americans by USA Lacrosse. first-team All-American Nate Kabiri leads the Tigers with 76 points on the season. He’s followed by third-team All-American Chad Palumbo with 67 points and a team-high 42 goals.

The Nittany Lions shut these two down for the most part in their earlier meeting but they each found pockets of success when the defense broke down, with Kabiri tallying two goals and an assist and Palumbo putting up a goal and an assist as well. If Penn State’s defense, and Hawkins isn’t as vigilant, on Sunday, the pair have the ability to run away with the game.

Hunter Spiess is a ground ball machine on defense, with a defense-high 50 pickups on the season. He’s also caused 14 turnovers, tied for second behind Jack Stahl’s 20 forced turnovers.

Ivy League Goaltender of the Year Ryan Croddick is Princeton’s last line of defense with a .578 save percentage and a 9.54 goals-against average. He had 10 saves in the loss to Penn State and should Croddick maintain his excellent form, he’ll once more be a thorn in the blue and white’s side.

title: Penn State men's gymnastics announces head coaching change
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Randy Jepson's 35 years as the head coach for Penn State, a leadership change has been announced for the program.

Penn State Athletics announced associate head coach Tony Beck will be the new head coach for the program after Jepson's contract wasn't renewed.

Thank you, Coach Jepson 🤝#WeAre pic.twitter.com/V1mPIypdat— Penn State Men’s Gymnastics (@PennStateMGYM) May 15, 2026

Jepson spent the last 45 years with Penn State --- first as an assistant coach before being elevated to head coach. During that time, he won three National Coach of the Year Awards and helped the Nittany Lions to three national titles.

At Penn State, Jepson coached his team to nearly 450 career victories and coached eight Olympians, most recently Stephen Nedoroscik.

He’s been a part of the program since 2008. Now he leads it.The seventh head coach in program history, Tony Beck.#WeAre pic.twitter.com/sNovSbFt3C— Penn State Men’s Gymnastics (@PennStateMGYM) May 15, 2026

After being a part of the program since 2008, Beck was elevated to head coach on Friday. Beck competed for the Nittany Lions from 2009 to 2013, earning multiple top finishes at the NCAA Championship.

As a coach, Beck won Pennsylvania Coach of the Year in 2015 at Centre Elite Gymnastics, before joining Penn State as a volunteer coach.

Beck was elevated to an assistant coach in 2016 and again promoted to associate head coach in 2023.

The Penn State alum will take over the program following a 3-3 record last season, including a last place finish at the Big Ten Championships.

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball star Angie Potthoff hired as chief of staff
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After graduating from the Lady Lions in 1997, Angie Potthoff is back in Happy Valley.

Potthoff was named chief of staff on Friday following the hiring of head coach Tanisha Wright on March 19.

During her days donning the blue and white, Potthoff became the 11th-best player in Penn State history, recording 1,725 points and 918 blocks in 68 starts.

After Penn State, Potthoff spent three years with the ABL and WNBA, as well as one season on the international stage with the U.S. World University Games.

Potthoff’s coaching career began at Indiana University of Pennsylvania, where she served as a graduate assistant, before becoming an assistant coach at Robert Morris. From 2002 to 2005, she was an assistant coach and eventually head coach at Beaver Area High School.

In 2006, her collegiate coaching career continued as she was named an assistant coach at Notre Dame.

After holding that position for three years, Potthoff became the associate director for the program for the next five years, before becoming the athletic director at Chartiers Valley School District.

In 2019, she returned to the Fighting Irish as director of operations for the women’s basketball program, before being named the associate director of name, image and likeness for the University’s athletics program.

title: Penn State earns commitment from 2027 QB Will Wood
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell’s got his quarterback for the Class of 2027.

Will Wood announced his commitment to Penn State via X on Friday.

The 3-star recruit per the 247Sports composite ranks as the No. 3 player out of Massachusetts and No. 29 quarterback in his class. He is so far the only quarterback to commit to Penn State in the Class of 2027.

The 6-foot-2, 220-pounder chose the Nittany Lions over Boston College, West Virginia and Arkansas.

Wood was offered by Penn State on April 20. The Nittany Lions were the second to last team to make him an offer, only followed by Maryland.

He doesn’t yet have an official visit scheduled, but has made unofficial trips out to State College and the coaching staff paid him a visit last week.

Wood finished his junior year at Xaverian Brothers High School by helping the Hawks capture their third-straight MIAA Division I State Championship. In the championship game against St. Johns Prep, Wood went 11-16 for 345 yards and five touchdowns.

title: Penn State baseball opens final regular-season series with run-rule win over Maryland
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
The Nittany Lions’ season is approaching its climax, although it’s been mostly catastrophic, especially on the road.

Staring one final series in the face, away against Maryland, Penn State needed all three wins to make a push for the Big Ten Tournament.

The trend of road woes flipped in Penn State's opener against the Terrapins, which started with a 13-3 win. The blue and white rose to 4-18 away from home on the year, taking advantage of a Maryland squad, which stood tied at the bottom of the Big Ten standings entering the clash.

For Penn State, coach Mike Gambino gave the pivotal start to righty Colin Fitzgerald, who, over 12 starts, posted a 6.45 ERA before stepping onto the bump. Going against the redshirt senior, and making the start for Maryland was righty redshirt junior James Gladden, who made his first start this season after making 13 relief appearances, which were rather harsh and left him with a 7.94 ERA.

Gladden’s start began as the stat line would suggest, with eight straight balls. Penn State struck first shortly after, with a ball hit to the warning track by third baseman Bryce Molinaro that almost carried out of the park with the heavy winds in Maryland, which still scored a sacrifice fly.

Cleanup bat and first baseman Kevin Karstetter choked up on a pitch from Gladden and sent a dribbling grounder to shortstop, which couldn’t be scooped up cleanly and allowed another run. Maryland ended the early threat via a double play soon after.

In the Terrapins’ first time up, Fitzgerald limited the harm to a single and a stolen base by star third baseman Brayden Martin.

After limited action, Maryland was stuck in the home half of the second, as Fitzgerald’s own control problems emerged. A leadoff 2-0 count led to a single, which only worsened when the runner advanced on a wild pitch. The Terrapins got on the board after Paul Jones II knocked an RBI single.

Penn State got a pair of outs, but not after a walk and a single loaded the bases. With two outs and the jam presented to him, Fitzgerald faced Martin and axed Maryland’s momentum.

The Terrapins turned to lefty Landon Edwards in the third inning. The leadoff batter, designated hitter Michael Anderson, got hit on the foot and stole second base, although that small ball became moot. Right fielder Jesse Jaconski knocked the first hit of the game, a two-out towering homer that carried deep left center, and pushed Penn State ahead 4-1.

The Terrapins generated their first real offensive production in the home half, after a leadoff single, second baseman David Mendez smashed a one-out home run to cut the score to 4-3. Fitzgerald notched back-to-back punchouts to bounce back and end the frame, which brought up the Penn State bats.

Catcher Avery Smith worked a leadoff walk, and centerfielder Maddox McDonald brought him home with his first dinger as a Nittany Lion, immediately undoing Maryland’s work at the plate.

Edwards was done on the mound after that, with righty Andrew Koshy entering for Maryland, but he immediately suffered a single by shortstop Preston Yaucher, marking Penn State’s first hit of the game that wasn’t a home run.

Penn State got back into its power-hitting ways, with Anderson launching a no-doubt-about-it home run, his 20th of the season, which marked a new program record. The round-tripper pushed him to the lone No. 2 in the Big Ten in long shots, only trailing top MLB Draft prospect Roch Cholowsky. That would be the extent of the action with the bats, as Penn State led 8-3.

Fitzgerald continued his work as his pitch count exceeded 90, and a loaded bases jam ended his day, which was brought on primarily by two walks. Righty Ben DeMell picked up the load with two outs and navigated to a full-count strikeout.

The top of the fifth started with a walk and a single by Penn State, with one scoring when McDonald launched a ball into the gap for a double. Yaucher got on base via a fielder’s choice followed by a walk, which loaded them up for Anderson. The slugger ripped a single through the left side of the infield to score two and push Penn State closer to a run-rule win.

Molinaro ripped a double over the left fielder’s head to keep the runs coming. After one scored, a second attempted the journey home but was narrowly caught out on a play that was sent to the review booth. After an eventful top of the fifth, Penn State led 12-3.

DeMell allowed two runners, but no runs, with two strikeouts, as Penn State inched closer to the win. Ryan Bailey took over on the mound for Maryland and allowed a double to the first batter he faced, Karstetter. The Nittany Lions pulled off a tricky baserunning play on an attempted steal to second to plate Karstetter, and went ahead 13-3.

Mason Horwat got the call to take over on the mound and worked a scoreless frame, which prompted Maryland to make its own pitching change, with Quinn Yellin entering. Penn State worked a pair of baserunners, but couldn’t send either of them home, as regardless only a half inning separated it from victory.

Horwat ended the Terrapins’ last batting chances, only allowing a pair of walks.

With the result, the Nittany Lions now stand at 15-34 on the season, with Big Ten Tournament hopes still alive.

Up next

The blue and white continue against Maryland with Game 2 of the series tomorrow, with the same first pitch time of 6 p.m. The action can be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

title: Penn State notebook | Jeff Tambroni talks Princeton, season-long growth ahead of NCAA Quarterfinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 8 Penn State is headed back to the NCAA Quarterfinals after defeating Army in the first round of the national tournament at Panzer Stadium. Now, the Big Ten champions are set to face the Ivy League’s best, No. 1 Princeton, at noon on Sunday in Newark, Delaware.

Ahead of the blue and white’s second-consecutive quarterfinal appearance, coach Jeff Tambroni met with the media over Zoom to discuss the upcoming elimination game as well as how the team is planning on putting its season-long growth to good use.

Scouting the Tigers

Penn State, having previously defeated Princeton in the Tigers’ season-opener, recognizes that its opponents aren’t the same team that they were in February. Tambroni understands that Princeton has gone through a lot of growth throughout the season which led to an Ivy League championship as well as the No. 1 ranking in the polls.

However, he isn’t looking at the game in terms of what the Tigers are doing. Rather, he’s focusing on his own squad and building their synergy to play as a team instead of specifically honing in on a set group of opposing players. Teamwork and communication is key for Tambroni’s gameplan.

“They have a tremendous amount of talent,” Tambroni said. “We have a ton of respect for the amount of talent that they have, and I think one of the things that we did in the first go-around is that I just thought we played really well together. I thought our offense and defense played really well in more of a collaborative sense, and I don't think you can take a different approach.”

As they’ve been over the course of the season, attackmen Nate Kabiri and Chad Palumbo present major threats to Penn State’s defense. But that’s not all. After having to solve Army’s Sean Byrne, now the Nittany Lions will face Ivy League Goaltender of the Year Ryan Croddick.

“It doesn't get any easier. If you look at where we were, Maryland, Johns Hopkins, Army and now Princeton — we've faced some really talented goalies,” Tambroni said. “But I think the recognition is that it doesn't get any easier in between the pipes and that we're going to have to do our best to generate as much or more higher scoring opportunities, and hope that we're going to have a little bit more success this week than we did last week in that regard.”

Growth over time

Princeton’s improvement since its season-opening loss at home hasn’t been echoed in the same way by Penn State, who’d continued to struggle with unranked teams despite wiping the floor with the likes of Yale and Cornell.

However, since a loss to Michigan on April 11, the Nittany Lions saw immense growth at the conclusion of the regular season and throughout the Big Ten tournament. Princeton is a different team and it’ll be looking to prove that by taking revenge on the only remaining team that’d beat it in the regular season, with the Big Red already eliminated.

“Certainly they will be very well prepared coming into this game with the experience that they've garnered throughout the course of the year, but I hope we will be as well,” Tambroni said.

Annapolis lingers

This time last year, the Nittany Lions faced Notre Dame in the NCAA Quarterfinals in Annapolis, Maryland. They mounted a six-goal comeback in the second half to move on to the national semifinal at Gillette Stadium.

While the team lost Matt Traynor, Kevin Parnham, Ethan Long and Jack Fracyon to graduation, Tambroni retained many of who’d made the comeback possible including Kyle Lehman, Hunter Aquino and Alex Ross.

The experience that was earned in that game, Tambroni said, would be crucial to the preparation aspect of the gameplan as “once the game starts, the advantages of ‘having been there’ end.”

“You could look at that in one of two ways,” Tambroni said. “There's one side of it that you don't need to see something in order to truly believe it. You can have a tremendous amount of success if you prepare hard. But at the same time, I think having been there is more useful in preparation. It does help in preparing you mentally and physically.”

title: Storylines to watch for Penn State track and field in Big Ten Championships
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three months of action that have produced numerous school records, personal bests and top 10 marks in school history, Penn State’s postseason is finally here.

The Nittany Lions will travel to Lincoln, Nebraska, for the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships from Friday to Sunday with the hopes of bringing home some hardware.

Here’s a look at some of the top storylines heading into this weekend’s star-studded action.

‘800m U’

For the last decade, Penn State has acquired the title of “800m U”, developing elite athletes Isaiah Harris and Handal Roban, as well as breaking the school record multiple times over the last few years.

The Nittany Lions took home gold on both the men’s and women’s side at the indoor conference championship earlier this season and will look to do it again at the outdoor meet.

On the men’s side, they’ll be without reigning indoor Big Ten champion Roban, who is redshirting the outdoor season. Instead, Nebraska transfer Niko Schultz, who has the No. 3 time in school history, will look to keep the 800-meter success alive.

Schultz ran a time of 1:45.24 at the Virginia Challenge earlier this season, good enough for No. 2 in the conference. However, graduate students Allon Clay and Yukichi Ishii will also be in the mix for the top spot, sitting fourth and ninth in school history and third and ninth in the conference.

On the women’s side, senior Hayley Kitching continued to be the Nittany Lions’ premiere mid-distance runner this season.

Kitching also set her season best at the Virginia Challenge, running 2:02.65 for sixth in the conference. Coming off an indoor season where she ran sub-two minutes for the first time in her career, she’ll be looking for another school record this season.

However, Kitching isn’t alone in the 800m. Freshman Boh Ritchie, sophomore Tayissa Buchanan and graduate student Allison Johnson each have top-10 marks in program history, with Ritchie coming off an indoor NCAA championship appearance.

Throwing records

In a new era of Penn State throws under Olympian Darrell Hill, the Nittany Lions have found a lot of success in less than a year.

The blue and white have a total of nine entries in the men’s and women’s hammer throw and shot put with a strong chance for podium finishes.

In the women’s shot put, the young duo of Skye Petrie-Cameron and Sasha Garnett have put themselves in the top 10 in school history and top 15 in the Big Ten. Petrie-Cameron, a freshman, threw a career-best 16.68 meters in the last meet of the season, while Garnett, a sophomore, threw 16.44 meters two weeks earlier.

However, veteran Alex Pancoast also leapt into the leaderboards in the last meet of the season, this time in the women’s hammer throw. The senior threw a five-meter personal best, throwing 60.09 meters for sixth in program history.

The men’s side has seen just as much success, as Ryan Henry and Dylan Lambrecht have continued to battle in the men’s shot put. Henry sits seventh in school history with a personal best 19.48 meters, while Lambrecht is eighth-best with 19.45 meters. Freshman Bryce Thompson has a personal best of 18.81 meters, just 0.08 meters shy of the 10th-best mark.

In the men’s hammer throw, graduate student Collin Burkhart has been a one-man wrecking crew. Burkhart sits ninth in the conference with a mark of 67.53 meters, good for third in school history.

Sprinting success

Penn State has only one athlete competing in the men’s 100-meter and one in the women’s 100m and 200-meter. However, the two are among the best in the nation.

After setting the school record at the Florida Relays with a commanding time of 10.05 seconds, sophomore Ajani Dwyer hasn’t competed in the 100m in the last month. However, he has the No. 3 mark in the conference and is likely looking for his first podium finish in his young career.

On the other hand, senior Marie-Eloise Leclair has had a very successful career in Division II. This weekend, she’ll have her first opportunity competing in the Division I postseason, but has had success all season.

Leclair will compete in both the 100m and 200m at the Big Ten championships, where she holds the No. 2 and No. 4 mark in school history.

The 2024 Olympian is coming off a world-qualifying relay performance with the Canadian national team earlier this month and a school record sprint medley relay the week before.

title: Penn State faculty vote 'yes' to unionize
author: Taylor Mcgillvray | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State faculty voted in favor of unionizing on Thursday, following a mail-in election administered by the Pennsylvania Labor Relations Board.

2,510 faculty members voted in favor of the union, which will represent thousands of faculty members across Penn State’s campuses, including both tenure-line and non tenure-line faculty.

The Penn State Faculty Alliance filed for a union election in December 2025, stating that a union would allow faculty to have a stronger voice in decisions regarding pay, benefits, working conditions and job security.

In a statement posted to the union's website, organizers said the outcome would help create a better working environment for education and also improve academic freedom for students.

description:
Savon Huggins has struck again in the Class of 2027.

3-star running back Chukwuma Odoh has committed to Penn State, he announced via Instagram.

Odoh ranks as the No. 734 player in the class, the No. 57 running back and the No. 22 player in New Jersey.

Penn State officially offered the running back on March 7, and Odoh prioritized the Nittany Lions, scheduling an official visit just 11 days later. He'll visit Happy Valley on June 19, and it’s the only official visit he’s scheduled.

Odoh had a star sophomore year in 2024, rushing for 1,418 yards and 16 touchdowns. He followed up that star year with a 629-yard rushing season while missing four games during the season.

title: 40 year anniversary special | Penn State’s 1986 national championship season through Collegian reporting
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s been a long 40 years since Penn State won its second and most recent national championship in 1986. Since then, Nittany Lions fans have been waiting for another title, coming close with a College Football Playoff semifinals appearance in 2024.

Boasting an undefeated 12-0 record, the 1986 Nittany Lions defeated two of the top teams in the country en route to a Fiesta Bowl victory, national title and No. 1 spot in the AP poll.

Here’s a look back at the team’s second national championship-winning season through Collegian reporting.

Season opener

The Nittany Lions began their path to greatness with a home opener against Temple, where they put on a show in front of 85,232 fans, winning 45-15.

Quarterback John Shaffer showed out in the opening three drives, helping the team find the end zone in each drive. The team captain finished the day with 194 yards, three passing touchdowns and a rushing score.

“I want to win football games and I want to be a part of a team that wins (football) games,” Shaffer said after the game.

Double-digit domination

After a 12-point victory over Boston College, Penn State returned to its dominant ways in blowout victories over East Carolina and Rutgers.

Penn State ended September with a 42-17 victory against the Pirates and found lots of offensive success with a 35-0 halftime lead. Collegian sports reporter Matt Herb wrote, “The Lions could have started their drives from inside the Ag Arena and still put the Pirates away by the end of the first quarter.”

“Everybody could see, they weren’t a good team,” linebacker Shane Conlan said after the game. “They have great athletes but they just didn’t put it together.”

A week later, Penn State put up another 25-point victory, this time against the Scarlet Knights, officially outscoring opponents 144-52 in its first four games of the season.

“Anytime our offense can put that many points on the board it helps us a lot,” safety Ray Isom said. “We know going out there that we have a lead, so we can just sit back and play cautious.”

Homecoming heroics

With over 85,000 fans and alumni in attendance, the Nittany Lions put on a deafening performance against Syracuse, winning 42-3 to improve to 6-0 on the season.

From the first play of the game, Penn State took control with an interception by Duffy Cobb. Off the turnover, fullback Tim Manoa ran 11 yards to the end zone for the score. While Manoa finished with 97 yards on the day, fellow running back Blair Thomas earned 92 yards in one play, falling just one yard short of the endzone. In all, Penn State tallied 434 yards on the ground.

Syracuse quarterback Don McPherson faced pressure from the Nittany Lion defense all game, as they recorded six sacks while holding the Orange to just 248 yards and a field goal. Leading receiver Scott Schwedes recorded only 13 yards.

“They played power football, came right at us and more or less said ‘stop us’,” Syracuse linebacker Roger Remo said. “I think in the first half we did a damn good job against them.”

Sweet home Alabama

For the first time that season, Penn State faced a ranked opponent in No. 2 Alabama. Defying odds, the Nittany Lions traveled to Tuscaloosa and came home with a 23-3 victory, becoming the No. 2-ranked team in the following week’s AP poll.

Penn State’s offense continued to be the difference-maker on the scoreboard, finding success both rushing and passing the ball.

Shaffer commanded the offense yet again to an 11-point halftime lead behind a 19-yard rushing touchdown by D.J. Dozier and a short double reverse by Thomas, who recorded 53 of the 77 yards on the drive. The rushing attack finished the day with 234 yards, while Shaffer recorded 168 yards in the air.

“I thought we had a good football team coming down here,” Paterno said. “I really thought we were better than Alabama.”

With its first ranked victory of the year, the Nittany Lions defeated Alabama at Bryant-Denny Stadium for just the third time in 25 years, ending a 12-0-1 unbeaten streak by the Tide.

Mo-town Shutout

While Penn State ultimately finished the season undefeated, it only recorded one shutout on the year. The Nittany Lions defeated West Virginia, 19-0, on the road to kickoff November.

Unlike the offensive domination that led the way through the other parts of the season, Penn State’s defense was on display against the Mountaineers, holding them to just 134 total yards, with only eight on the ground.

West Virginia too had a strong defensive performance, restricting the Nittany Lions’ high-powered offense to just 19 points and sacking Shaffer for 48 yards.

“I thought our defense played a very strong, tough game,” Paterno said. “We were obviously very inconsistent when we got into scoring areas, but we had too many opportunities to feel good about the game.”

Penn State only found the endzone once against the Mountaineers, as Shaffer found Dozier for a 23-yard strike. It was kicker Massimo Manca who scored the rest of the points, including a game-long 42-yard field goal.

Rivalry stretch

With two games left before Penn State claimed an undefeated regular season, the Nittany Lions were threatened with a late Notre Dame comeback in a battle between two historic rivals.

The Nittany Lions emerged victorious after a back-and-forth affair with Penn State taking the lead on back-to-back Shaffer scores — one in the air and one on the ground. The Fighting Irish didn’t go down without a fight, however, as quarterback’s Steve Beuerlein’s pass to tight end Joel Williams with a minute left fell short of a potential game-winning touchdown.

“I thought it was a great football game, a great effort by both quads and I’ve said it’s a shame that anybody had to lose,” Paterno said. “I think we played about as well as we could. I think they played as well as they could.”

The following week, the Nittany Lions claimed their second straight undefeated regular season with a 34-14 victory over in-state rival Pitt. It wasn’t much of a battle, as the Panthers only led for 16 seconds.

Following Pitt’s go-ahead touchdown, Thomas returned the kickoff 91 yards for the score before Dozier followed it up with another touchdown on the ground. Even with second-stringers playing in the second half, Matt Knizner found Eric Hamilton for an 82-yard passing touchdown as the lead piled up.

“I’ve never had a bunch of seniors who have been more committed who have made more personal sacrifices, worked harder with one goal in mind — that they want another shot at a national championship,” Paterno said. “Now they're going to get another shot at it.”

Victory, at last

For just the second time that season, Penn State faced a ranked opponent in No. 1 Miami for the national title in the Fiesta Bowl.

Despite being the underdog against the Hurricanes, the Nittany Lions kept things even heading into the final 15 minutes. Both sides had just one touchdown on the board, with Penn State’s coming via Shaffer’s legs.

After Miami took the lead again with a field goal, Penn State found its first lead of the game off a six-yard rushing touchdown by Dozier.

As the Hurricane marched back down the field with a chance to win the game, cornerback Pete Giftopoulos stepped in front of star receiver Brett Perriman, recording his second interception of the game to clinch the victory and Penn State’s second national championship in school history.

The Nittany Lions finished the season 12-0 as the No. 1 team in the country.

“I told them prior to the football game (that) they were a better football team, just remember that,” Paterno said. “It’s our football game, our national championship, just go get it.”

title: NCAA announces change to Division I men's soccer season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A new wrinkle is being put in place for the men's soccer season in 2027.

The NCAA sport's oversight committee adopted legislation to split the NCAA Division I men's soccer season between the fall and spring, moving the national tournament to the spring, starting in 2027.

Committee adopts 2-semester playing season for DI men’s soccer. https://t.co/jznRxJSURX— NCAA News (@NCAA_PR) May 13, 2026

The new changes would be put in place for the 2027-28 season, creating up to 18 matches during the current season with an additional 10 matches being played from mid-February to the start of the tournament.

The dates for the tournament will be determined later, while no changes are expected to the women's schedule.

The split schedule would also adjust the transfer window to one 15-consecutive day period in the spring, as opposed to the two windows for 45 days that are currently in place.

title: Penn State to lease DuBois campus facilities
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has entered into a transitional lease agreement with Mount Aloysius College, a private four-year liberal arts college located in Cresson, Pennsylvania, for the partial use of Penn State DuBois facilities, according to an official statement from the university.

The agreement demarcates a specific portion of classroom and office space in the DuBois Swift Building for transitional use through spring 2027 beginning July 1. While the closure timeline for Penn State Dubois will proceed as planned, Penn State will be the property owner and landlord in the interim, and will consider any long-term use of the property separately from this agreement.

Mount Aloysius will be an independently operating tenant within the Swift Building, and will offer “its own academic programs, faculty and student services under its existing accreditation and governance structure,” according to the statement.

Jungwoo Ryoo, chancellor and chief academic officer of Penn State DuBois, said in the statement that instruction, advising and other campus services “will continue as planned.”

Penn State DuBois is one of seven Commonwealth campuses Penn State plans to close after the 2026-27 school year.

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: 5 home runs not enough as Penn State baseball falls to Pitt
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the end of the season draws closer, Penn State sandwiched a standalone matchup with Pitt between the final two series of Big Ten play.

Despite hitting five home runs in the clash with the Panthers, the blue and white couldn’t match the Panthers’ offensive output and fell 11-7 in Pittsburgh.

Jayden Davis knocked a leadoff double to open the top of the first for Penn State, but the rest of the inning’s batters weren’t able to bring him home, sending the Panthers to the plate.

A huge inning for Pitt started with Kai Wagner singling to center field, sending a run home from third base to open the scoring. Another single from Trey Fenderson on the next at-bat brought another run home, putting the Panthers up 2-0 with just a single out in the inning.

The Panthers worked the bases loaded, and a deep single from Sebastian Pisacreta sent two more runners across the plate. The Nittany Lion defense eventually stopped the bleeding, ending the opening frame with Pitt leading 4-0.

Penn State got a run back in the top of the second, when Maddox McDonald hit a sacrifice fly that brought home Jesse Jaconski. The single run was all they could get in the inning, cutting the lead to 4-1.

Pitt had another high-scoring inning in their next turn at the dish, as a base-clearing double from Carter Dierdorf brought in three more runs. The Panthers loaded the bases again, and an ensuing walk made it an 8-1 lead for the squad.

After a second consecutive four-run inning from Pitt, Penn State delivered a second consecutive one-run inning in the bottom of the second when Bryce Molinaro powered his 15th home run of the season over the fence in right field.

Pitt added on two more runs in the top of the third, off a single from Fenderson and a sacrifice fly from Pisacreta, bringing the score to 10-2 in favor of the Panthers.

Back-to-back home runs from Spencer Barnett and Avery Smith cut into the Panthers’ lead in the top of the fourth inning, and after a runner reached base not long after, Davis crushed another to notch the Nittany Lions’ sixth run of the game.

The Penn State defense was able to hold Pitt scoreless for the first time in the bottom of the frame, sending the matchup into the fifth with the Panthers up 10-6.

Leading off at the top of the inning, Barnett hit his second round-tripper of the game to cut the Pitt advantage to just three runs. In the bottom of the fifth, the Nittany Lions held the Panthers scoreless again.

Molinaro singled in the top of the sixth inning, but the blue and white couldn’t drive him home. Mason Ligenza fired a solo shot in the bottom of the frame to make the score 11-7 as the teams headed into the seventh.

After a high-scoring first six innings, the final three saw the Nittany Lions and Panthers trade defensive stands and singles that didn’t result in any runs. Pitt held their four-run advantage throughout the rest of the game, sealing an 11-7 win and adding another loss to Penn State’s record.

The blue and white moved to 15-33 on the year, with a single series left to play, still on the outside of qualifying for the Big Ten Tournament.

Up Next

Penn State will open the regular season’s final series against Maryland on Thursday, with first pitch set for 6 p.m. at Bob “Turtle” Smith Stadium.

title: Climb Nittany to close May 31, citing unsustainable costs
author: Aly Cromar | The Daily Collegian
description:
After years of providing a climbing gym to the State College community, Climb Nittany will permanently shut its doors on May 31 at 6 p.m.

The gym cited rent and occupancy costs, which they called unsustainable in a statement posted Sunday to its website and social media accounts. The owners looked at ways to restructure the business before settling on closure, the statement read.

Climb Nittany opened in late 2020 as the first Pennsylvania location for 5.Life, an Ohio-based gym company. The closure leaves Centre County without a dedicated indoor rock climbing gym.

Members with punch passes or unused annual memberships will receive refunds by June 2. Monthly memberships stopped renewing as of May 10.

title: Rally House Retailers acquires Lion & Cub
author: Aly Cromar
description:
Rally House, the largest retailer of collegiate goods and apparel, is set to take over Lion & Cub, a longtime Penn State staple, in early June.

The retailer announced the acquisition in a press release on Tuesday. Located at 206 E. College Ave, the store has served as a popular spot to purchase Penn State merchandise since 1975.

According to the press release, Lion & Cub owner Benson Lichtig is “proud to pass the business to Rally House” as he prepares for retirement.

Rally House CEO Aaron Liebert said in the release that the company plans to build on Lion & Cub’s reputation while expanding where they are able, in order to continue the store's legacy.

Along with Lion & Cub, Rally House plans to open a second location on North Atherton Street in the fall.
