title: Former Penn State players get initial NFL numbers, rookie contracts revealed
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
All of Penn State’s 2026 draftees have had their initial NFL numbers revealed ahead of rookie minicamp.

Baltimore Ravens first-round pick Vega Ioane will wear No. 71, the same number he wore at Penn State. Ioane signed a 4-year, $24.2 million deal with a $14 million signing bonus.

Former Penn State quarterback Drew Allar will wear No. 16 for the Pittsburgh Steelers. Allar's number with the Nittany Lions, No. 15, was unavailable, as it's currently being worn by wide receiver Ben Skowronek.

Dallas Cowboys draftee Drew Shelton will don No. 67, signing a 4-year, $5.575 million contract. Similarly, Green Bay Packers draft pick, Dani Dennis-Sutton inked a 4-year, $5.53 million deal with a $1.15 million signing bonus. Dennis-Sutton is set to wear No. 51.

Nick Singleton will wear No. 32 for the Tennessee Titans, while Kaytron Allen will don No. 31, the reversal of his No. 13 with the Nittany Lions.

Defensive linemen Zane Durant will wear No. 92 for the Buffalo Bills, and Carolina Panthers draft pick Zakee Wheatley will wear No. 38.

Undrafted free agent signee Tyler Duzansky signed with the Las Vegas Raiders, earning a 3-year, 3.11 million deal with $110,000 guaranteed and a $10,000 signing bonus. He is wearing No. 46, one digit lower than his number at Penn State.

Other undrafted free agents include Nick Dawkins and Dom DeLuca, who both signed with the Baltimore Ravens. Dawkins will wear No. 53 and DeLuca will wear No. 42.

title: Essential information for spring 2026 commencement weekend
author: Michelle Diamond | News Editor
description:
As students and families are preparing for spring 2026 commencement, which takes place from May 8 to May 10 in the Bryce Jordan Center, here’s some information to help you navigate the weekend.

Commencement Schedule

Friday, May 8

The Schreyer Honors College Medals Ceremony will begin at 4 p.m in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The College of Engineering commencement ceremony will begin at 7 p.m in the Bryce Jordan Center.

Saturday, May 9

9 a.m

The Smeal College of Business commencement ceremony will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The College of Information Sciences and Technology commencement ceremony will be in the Pegula Ice Arena.

1:30 p.m

The Bellisario College of Communications commencement ceremony will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The College of Earth and Mineral Sciences commencement ceremony will be in the Pegula Ice Arena.

The College of Arts and Architecture commencement ceremony will be in the Eisenhower Auditorium.

5 p.m.

The College of the Liberal Arts commencement ceremony will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The Ross and Carol Nese College of Nursing commencement ceremony will be in the Eisenhower Auditorium.

Sunday, May 10

9 a.m.

The College of Health and Human Development commencement ceremony will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The College of Education commencement ceremony will be in the Pegula Ice Arena.

1:30 p.m.

The Eberly College of Science commencement ceremony will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The College of Agricultural Sciences commencement ceremony will be in the Pegula Ice Arena.

5 p.m.

The J. Jeffrey and Ann Marie Fox Graduate School commencement ceremony for master's candidates will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

The J. Jeffrey and Ann Marie Fox Graduate School commencement ceremony for doctoral candidates will be in the Bryce Jordan Center.

A livestream of the event will be available for each college’s commencement. To access the livestreams, visit commencement.psu.edu. Recordings will be archived and become available for viewing after commencement.

Parking information

Attendees can park in the following lots: Jordan East, Stadium West and Porter North.

Designated sections of Jordan East and Stadium West will be for ADA parking. The locations are near Gate B of the Bryce Jordan Center, Orange L lot and the row of Stadium West along Curtin Road. Guest Relations, located inside Gate B, will be there to assist with accessibility needs.

Road closures

There are two road closures that could affect attendees’ routes.

Innovation Park/Penn State University exit is closed.

I-80 Westbound exit 161 (Bellefonte) is closed.

What you can and can’t bring

Guests can bring clear plastic, vinyl or PVC bags no larger than 12" x 6" x 12", one-gallon clear plastic freezer bags or small clutch bags no larger than 4.5" x 6.5" x 1". Bags are limited to one per guest.

Flowers, empty refillable water bottles, umbrellas, strollers, walkers, wheelchairs and other mobility assistance devices are permitted. Cameras and flash photography are also permitted.

Prohibited items include backpacks, diaper bags, messenger bags, duffel bags, roll bags, binocular cases, coolers and tri-pods set up in the aisle or that block other guests' views. There will be no check-in for prohibited items or bags.

description:

title: Kickoff time for Penn State at Northwestern announced
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's schedule is continuing to form.

The Nittany Lions will play Northwestern on Fox at 8 p.m. on Friday, Oct. 2, the network announced Friday.

It's been seven years since Penn State has played on a Friday, last playing in 2019 against Maryland. This season, Fox is replacing its Friday night WWE Smackdown with a 12-game slate for Fox College Football Friday.

title: Penn State softball falls to UCLA in quarterfinals, eliminated from Big Ten Tournament
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After pulling off an upset win over Northwestern on Wednesday, Penn State advanced to the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals for a matchup against No. 3 UCLA. With a spot in the semifinals on the line, the Nittany Lions faced one of the toughest tests of their season.

After early-game offense from the Bruins, Penn State fell to UCLA 6-1, ending the season in their second postseason game.

Senior right-hander Taylor Tinsley took the circle for the Bruins, though Breanna Hanik wasted no time getting Penn State going with a leadoff single. Despite the knock, the Bruins limited the Nittany Lions to no runs in the top of the first.

Junior right-hander McKenna Young got the starting nod for the blue and white. Rylee Slimp started it off with a drive to left field, but after a diving play by Hanik, she was out.

The Bruins got to work after that, as Megan Grant hit a solo home run off the first pitch, giving UCLA a 1-0 lead to start the bottom of the first.

It didn’t stop there, as Jordan Woolery hit a line-drive double. Aleena Garcia stepped to the plate next and immediately hit a two-run home run, making the score 3-0.

With UCLA on fire, Bri Alejandre continued it with a solo home run, extending the Bruin lead to four.

Coach Clarisa Crowell relieved Young and put in junior Bridget Nemeth to finish the first. The Bruins were unable to get anything going after that and finished the inning with four runs.

The second inning was quiet for both teams as both defenses locked down each other's offenses. The third was another quiet inning for Penn State, as they were left runless again.

It was not the same for the Bruins, as Alexis Ramirez started it off with a solo home run, making the score 5-0.

Soo-Jin Berry took to the plate and hit a double, keeping up the momentum for the Bruins. Jolyna Lamar took the opportunity to hit it deep into the outfield, sending Berry home on an RBI triple and giving UCLA a 6-0 lead.

Penn State swapped pitchers again, this time to freshman Brevyn Kellepouris, who closed out the inning.

Both pitchers showed their dominance in the fourth. Tinsley struck out two hitters, and Kellepouris struck out one. Tinsley, at this point in the game, has struck out five runners.

It was not only the pitchers who put their dominance on display, but also both defenses, as they made crucial catches.

UCLA ace Tinsley kept racking up the strikeouts, as she struck out three more hitters in the fifth. The only production from the Nittany Lions came from Allison Oneacre with a double and Brooke Klosowicz with a walk.

Lamar opened the fifth with a double, trying to rev up the rally for UCLA. This worked as both Grant and Woolery earned a walk, leaving the bases loaded.

As Garcia stepped to the plate, she hit it to third, but Oneacre caught it and threw it to first, earning two outs to end the inning. This play allowed Penn State to avoid a run-rule walkoff.

The Nittany Lions were not backing down, showing the determination they have had all season.

Frankee Flesher led off the sixth with a single, turning up the momentum for the Nittany Lions, followed by another single from Michela Barbanente, but Flesher got out. Keeping the blue and white in the game, Gradishar earned a walk.

The Nittany Lions weren’t to be counted out, as Walters bunted the ball and made it to first, allowing Barbanente to make it home. The score was 6-1 with only one inning to go.

The fight continued as the Penn State defense got 1-2-3 inning for the first time in the game.

Penn State could get nothing going in the seventh and fell to UCLA in the quarterfinals, ending the season for the Nittany Lions.

title: A look back at Drew Allar’s receiving targets during his Penn State career
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Drew Allar entered the 2026 NFL Draft process with a lot of question marks and was seen as more of a project with a higher ceiling. But the Pittsburgh Steelers took a chance on his potential, drafting him in the third round as their potential quarterback of the future.

While he had certain flaws that were from his game specifically, the receiving room didn’t help him develop into the quarterback that many thought he could become.

Allar joins a quarterback room for the Steelers that features former Ohio State quarterback Will Howard, who was throwing to multiple first-round picks and NFL impact makers.

With Allar’s Penn State career officially over, here’s a look at the talent he was throwing to.

Outlier - Tyler Warren

The outlier of the group during Allar’s time was Tyler Warren. The now-Indianapolis Colt was Allar’s top target and it was no coincidence that the former Penn State quarterback’s best year was with Warren as the top weapon for the Nittany Lions.

In Warren’s final season with Penn State, he caught 104 passes for 1,233 yards and eight touchdowns in 2025. Warren proved his final year wasn’t a fluke after getting drafted in the first round to the Colts and quickly becoming a go-to target for Indianapolis’ quarterback Daniel Jones.

Warren caught 76 passes for 817 yards and four touchdowns in his rookie season, cementing himself as the best target Allar had during his time in Happy Valley.

Top wide receivers

While Warren was a reliable target in 2025, the wide receivers during Allar’s three years were a different story.

The top receiving target each year was KeAndre Lambert-Smith in 2023, Harrison Wallace III in 2024 and Trebor Peña in 2025. After their leading seasons, Lambert-Smith and Wallace both transferred to SEC schools.

Lambert-Smith’s biggest moment at Penn State is most notably from Allar’s first touchdown pass of his career as a starter in Penn State’s home-opener against West Virginia. Lambert-Smith went on to have a successful showing at Auburn with 981 yards and eight touchdowns, resulting in the Los Angeles Chargers taking him in the fifth round.

Lambert-Smith is the lone wide receiver Allar played with as a starter to be drafted. Despite getting action with the Chargers, Lambert-Smith was a late-round pick, which wasn’t up to even Penn State’s standards.

The Nittany Lions were a team consistently sending wide receivers to the NFL when Sean Clifford was the quarterback, including KJ Hamler, Jahan Dotson and Parker Washington. But with a significantly better quarterback prospect, the wide receiver talent significantly dropped off.

A year later, Wallace did the same thing, transferring to Ole Miss following an unproductive season as the top receiver. Similarly to Lambert-Smith, Wallace’s best game as Penn State’s No. 1 wide receiver came against West Virginia, when he caught five passes for 117 yards and two touchdowns. That production significantly dropped as he caught just two more touchdowns the rest of the 2024 season.

While Wallace went on to have a successful season at Ole Miss, he didn’t produce with the Nittany Lions like the top target of a national-champion contending team typically would, including not catching a single pass during Penn State’s biggest game in recent years against Notre Dame in the College Football Playoff semifinal.

In Allar’s final year, a season where expectations were at an all-time high, his top wide receiver in terms of yards was Peña, who totaled just 552 yards. However, a big chunk of those yards were with Ethan Grunkemeyer at the helm.

Despite the focus to add receivers in the transfer portal — Penn State brought in Peña, Devonte Ross and Kyron Hudson — they didn’t produce at the level they were expected to. All three went undrafted this April alongside Wallace.

No matter the year, Allar was severely hindered due to his wide receivers’ overall struggle to create separation, often causing the former Nittany Lion gunslinger to fit a ball into a tight window.

Deep threats

The secondary targets for Allar didn’t produce at the level Penn State needed to help Allar become the 5-star quarterback he was recruited to be.

One of Allar’s biggest traits is his arm strength. But while Penn State had Omari Evans and Ross in 2024 and 2025, respectively, they weren’t as much of a threat, only connecting every so often on a deep ball to the deep-threat targets.

While there may not have been an effort to push the ball down the field, Ross and Evans were the primary receivers thrown to when there were opportunities. However, they didn’t make enough of an impact for Allar’s deep ball to be a big strength heading into his draft evaluation.

Evans transferred to Washington for the 2025 season and also wasn’t drafted, signing with the Kansas City Chiefs as an undrafted free agent.

Other tight ends

Following Warren’s departure to the NFL, Luke Reynolds was expected to step in to become the next big tight end in 2025.

In his first year as the starter, Reynolds didn’t live up to those high expectations, catching just 26 passes for 257 yards. Although no one thought he’d match Warren’s numbers from 2025, that’s a significant drop off from what was supposed to be the top tight end target.

The other tight end who made an impact was Khalil Dinkins, but he was used more as a blocking tight end, totaling only 167 yards.

The running backs were the main focal point during Allar’s tenure as the starting quarterback, but his receiving targets outside of Warren were lackluster, not allowing him to fully develop into the superstar quarterback he potentially could be.

He may not start right away with the Steelers, but if, or when, he does, he’ll have the receiving threats to be able to let the ball fly, potentially allowing him to break out into a highly productive quarterback.

title: Polish Water Ice brings soft serves and stadium classics to downtown State College
author: Justin Elliott | For the Collegian
description:
Downtown State College has officially welcomed a new, sweet treat. Located on 127 W. Beaver Ave., Polish Water Ice is a hybrid eatery, complete with a variety of soft-serve treats and stadium food favorites.

Polish Water Ice has a wide array of inclusive ice cream and water ice options, including oat-based, vegan-friendly and gluten-free ice cream, as well as Hershey’s ice cream.

Their signature soft serve, Polish water ice, will come in more than 20 fat-free, dairy-free and gluten-free flavors. General Manager Justin Wortman said they should be available in early June, barring any setbacks regarding county regulations, but the rest of the menu is available today.

Wortman, a big Penn State football fan, said he is familiar with the foods offered at popular State College venues like Beaver Stadium, Medlar Field and the Bryce Jordan Center.

He said the shop will be “PSU-themed” and use the same ingredients as these venues at exclusive prices, offering hot dogs, fries, sandwiches, cheesesteaks and chicken tender baskets.

“This is a place where you can come and have great foods at a better price,” Wortman said.

Polish Water Ice accepts LionCash from students and is also available on DoorDash for delivery. It’s open every day besides Mondays and is currently running a free small ice cream special for customers that join their free loyalty program.

title: Penn State Canvas down after 'global cybersecurity incident'
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This is a developing story.

Penn State Canvas is temporarily down after a message was sent earlier on Thursday saying the application was the subject of a “global cybersecurity incident.”

Over 9,000 universities are affected by the incident, according to the Duke Chronicle. ShinyHunters — a hacker group known for large-scale data breaches, claimed responsibility for the incident, said the Daily Pennsylvanian.

The group wrote in a message to affected Canvas pages that any university that does not want its data released should contact the group before the end of day on May 12. The message also stated that ShinyHunters had breached Instructure's systems previously.

At 5:13 p.m., Penn State sent an Outlook email saying Canvas is "experiencing a disruption to Canvas related to an ongoing security incident affecting Instructure, the vendor that provides the Canvas platform." The email also said users should avoid clicking on links, messages or content that may be posted by threat actors.

Canvas is currently displaying a message that says it is "undergoing scheduled maintenance."

title: NCAA to expand men's, women's Division I basketball tournament to 76 teams
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Changes are officially happening to the college basketball postseason.

The NCAA is expanding the men's and women's basketball tournaments to from 68 to 76 teams beginning in 2027 as announced Thursday.

NCAA reveals new 76-team bracket for DI Men’s and Women’s Basketball Championships. https://t.co/VkZ0uG67yk— NCAA News (@NCAA_PR) May 7, 2026

The additional teams will compete in the Opening Round games during the first week of each tournament. With more games, the NCAA said it will be able to award more than $131 million in revenue distribution to participating schools.

With the additional spots in each tournament, there will be more at-large squads earning a bid. The change means that 21% of Division I college basketball teams across the nation will be included.

The NCAA said these changes to its tournament will not impact the regular season or conference championship schedules.

For Penn State specifically, the change to a 76-team field might not affect the program immediately as the men's team went 12-20 while the women's team went 11-18 last season, causing them both to miss the tournament.

That said, if either squad sees improvements in the future, both Penn State teams would have better chances at securing an at-large bid due to more bids being available.

title: Black student groups are building the mental health support they can’t always find elsewhere
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Students of color often experience unique mental health challenges that are augmented by race and discrimination, which sometimes means traditional counseling may prove less effective for them.

According to the Penn State Center for Collegiate Mental Health’s 2023 report, nearly 20% of students entering counseling said they had experienced discrimination within the past six months. Those students reported higher levels of distress, social isolation and suicidal ideation than their peers.

While students who reported discrimination improved during counseling at similar rates to their peers, the report found they “consistently ended treatment with higher average levels of distress, demonstrating a persistent outcome disparity.”

At Penn State, students say these gaps have widened amid ongoing DEI rollbacks and a campus climate where many feel unheard. But Black students have stepped in to create their own mental health support through student-run programming within their clubs and Greek letter organizations.

Members of the Gamma Nu chapter of Alpha Phi Alpha describe the fraternity as a space that has impacted their well being.

“My mental health definitely grew stronger, I feel, (since) joining the Alphas,” Kai Holden, a fourth-year student studying cybersecurity analytics and operations, said. “...I gained just a strong support system of all types of brothers.”

Other members described similar experiences, pointing to the brotherhood as both an emotional support system and a source of mentorship.

“The brotherhood is really a strong support system for me, and then it's also a good network of examples that I have,” Nicholas Morrison said. “If I'm facing a problem, I know somebody who's been through something similar, and they can give me some good advice, kind of help me through that, and then just overall kind of fortify me.”

A 2019 study published in the American Journal of Qualitative Research found that Black Greek-letter fraternities “significantly impacts Black men’s college experiences at a predominantly white institution” by fostering personal growth and a strong support network. The study cites earlier research showing that Black student organizations function as “agents for social integration of students of color” at PWIs.

Holden, chapter president of the Gamma Nu chapter, said the brothers extend this support beyond their fraternity through mental-health-oriented events, such as annual ones like “Brother, You’re on my Mind” or stand-alones like the “Worldwide Day of Reflection.”

“As college students, especially for those who are living alone and living away from their parents, there’s not a lot of outlets to be able to just communicate certain feelings with everything going on in the world,” Holden said. “We try to host events that are able to just allow you to express how you feel.”

The Worldwide Day of Reflection event sectioned students off into different rooms where they could discuss how they’re personally feeling — from school stress to the state of the world. Another room had games aimed as a more relaxed outside for students to alleviate stress.

Additionally, Gamma Nu holds reactionary events. Any world circumstance — like a nearby school shooting, Holden said — that appears to widely impact the community is when they feel the need to hold extra events to “make sure everyone is OK.”

With DEI now a politically hot topic under the Trump administration, he said students can feel like they’re not getting the same support as they were before.

“Think about how that feels for a minority college student that just got to college, and they’re applying to these internships, and these companies are saying, ‘Oh, yeah, we don’t do that DEI stuff anymore,” Holden said. “It’s a very stressful situation for a lot of people now.”

One common theme students address during these discussions is the feeling of being “silenced.” They often mention feeling like they’re not able to voice what they’re feeling.

“Especially as a minority as well, it’s that feeling of being surrounded and just not being heard at all,” Holden said. “The real combat to that is really just emphasizing the fact that we’re not alone … showing them they do have a voice, and as a group, we are heard.”

Because Gamma Nu centers these events around community building, students feel less isolated in their struggles when they see others candidly talking about their own. Holden noted substantial differences in how participants approach their mental health. Engagement in these events has made students more willing to open up, he said.

He added that attendance has grown with each event, especially as they continue collaborating with other campus organizations, like Black Caucus and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People.

“It’s not just that we're just hosting the program, but people are engaging, people feel comfortable, and we're able to make that space for those people,” Morrison, a third-year studying architectural engineering, said.

Other student groups have taken similar approaches. Simone Warren, vice president of the Multicultural Women’s Forum (MWF), pointed to a number of ways for Black students to find community at Penn State, including the Student Black Caucus, Caribbean Student Association and more.

“There’s so many different ways for Black students to get connected,” Warren, a third-year studying psychology, said.

For Warren, joining MWF, which is housed within the Smeal College of Business and focuses on “academic and personal issues specific to women of color,” has strengthened both her mental health and her identity.

She said it’s hard to feel comfortable at Penn State as a woman of color at times, and MWF has helped her navigate that.

“Joining MWF was probably one of the best decisions I made my freshman year,” Warren said. “...Coming from a more predominantly Black area to a PWI was a very big jump… it really helped me develop a sense of belonging.”

A 2018 study published in the Journal of Research in Scientific Teaching, which interviewed 39 women of color in STEM to describe how counterspaces, including both STEM and non-STEM campus groups, found that many leave these predominantly white, male-dominated fields because they “do not experience a sense of social belonging.” Some remain, the study says, “due, in part, to counterspaces in which they participate.” MWF functions as the same kind of counterspace.

While Graham described how transformative community-driven solutions have been for her, she acknowledges that clinical care is just as essential, making the need for culturally competent counseling to close the outcome gap even more impressive.

“I think it's great to build a sense of community on your campus and feel more connected to the people around you, but it's also important to have someone who's more clinically and professionally trained,” Warren said. “I feel like one should never fully replace the other. They should go hand in hand, because they'll help you in different ways. I think both are equally very important.”

Editor's note: This story is part of The Daily Collegian’s participation in the Solutions Journalism Network’s Student Media Challenge. This year’s cohort focused specifically on mental health. Solutions journalism reports not only on widely shared problems, but also on how individuals and communities are working to address them.

title: Penn State softball upsets Northwestern, advances in Big Ten Tournament
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State opened postseason play on Wednesday against Northwestern in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament, looking to keep the season alive and punch a ticket to the next round.

The Nittany Lions advanced to the quarter finals with a 11-5 win over the Wildcats.

Northwestern turned to freshman right-hander Marina Mason in the circle to open tournament play. Mason immediately earned two strikeouts against Kaylie Walters and Allison Oneacre.

The Nittany Lions were left scoreless in the first as it was a quick three and out.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton got the starting nod from the Nittany Lions. Northwestern’s Gracie Nieto worked a 10-pitch at bat against Britton and earned a walk to start off.

With two outs on the board, Nieto stole second and Kansas Robinson earned a walk. A wild pitch against Bridget Donahey allowed Robinson to steal second and Nieto to steal third.

Donahey proceeded to hit a three-run home run to put the Wildcats up 3-0 early in the game.

Nothing was working for the blue and white to start the second, as all they ended the half inning was a walk by Natalie Lieto and a hit by Frankee Flesher.

Northwestern came alive in the second as Isabel Cunnea started with a solo home run, putting the Wildcats up 4-0.

Coach Clarisa Crowell called for a pitching change, putting in freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris, who proceeded to strikeout Teagan McCue to start the momentum for the Nittany Lions. Northwestern couldn’t get anything else going in the second.

The blue and white stepped it up in the third, as Gabby Gradishar opened it with an infield hit for a single.

Breanna Hanik stepped to the plate with two outs on the board and hit a two run home run, getting Penn State on the board 4-2 to end the third.

The Nittany Lions defense dominated in the third, allowing no Wildcat runs to cross home plate.

The Penn State offense’s dominance continued leading into the fourth, as Jordan George pinch hit for Flesher. George stepped to the plate and hit the ball towards third base to record a single.

After the next two outs, Gradishar hit another two run home run for Penn State, tying the game 4-4.

The Wildcats stepped it up in the bottom of the fourth as Tru Medina hit it to the outfield, almost getting a home run but ultimately settling for a double. Nieto followed up with a groundball up the middle to notch a single.

Kaylie Avvisato stepped to the plate and grounded out to Kellepouris. Despite the out, it allowed Medina to make it home, putting another Wildcat run on the board. Northwestern went back on top with a 5-4 lead against Penn State.

The blue and white fought back in the fifth, as Hanik hit it to left field for a single. Brooke Klosowicz struck out, but Hanik took an opportunity to steal second. It didn’t end there, as Lieto hit the Nittany Lions’ third two-run home run of the game to Penn State on top 6-5.

Most of Penn State’s runs at this point in the game came when they had two outs on the board.

The Nittany Lions switched pitchers again, this time going to McKenna Young to relieve Kellepouris.

Robinson led off the fifth with a double as pinch runner Kate Renschen stepped in for Robinson at second.

Donahey stepped up to the plate and hit a ball into the outfield, attempting to make it to second base, but a diving tag by Michela Barbanente saw her get out at second.

Northwestern was held to another scoreless inning.

The sixth inning was no different for the Nittany Lions, as Barbanente led off with a double. With a follow up two-run home run by Madison Seidel, the Penn State lead jumped to 8-5. This was the fourth home run of the game for the blue and white.

The Wildcats relieved Mason and put junior right-hander Renae Cunningham into the game

An action packed top of the sixth for the Nittany Lions as they proceeded to get 3 more runs, bringing up the lead 11-5.

Young put up a masterclass performance in the sixth and allowed no runs for Northwestern.

Momentum slowed down as the blue and white got no runs in the seventh, while Northwestern offered sophomore Signe Dohse onto the mound.

Young closed up the seventh with no runs, and Penn State defeated Northwestern.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions advance to the Big Ten quarterfinal, where they will go against No. 3 UCLA on Thursday at 7 p.m.

title: 3 questions Penn State needs to answer following spring ball
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrapped up the 15th practice of the spring on April 25 with Blue-White Practice, giving fans the first look at the Matt Campbell-led Nittany Lions.

In the week to follow, the team individually met with Campbell and their respective position coach, honing in on specific areas to grow over the summer months.

With the team entering an off period until team practice resumes in June, here’s a look at the biggest questions looming for Penn State following spring ball.

What’s the quarterback depth behind Rocco Becht?

Heading into spring, Iowa State transfer Rocco Becht was without a doubt going to be the starter in Week 1, followed by fellow former Cyclone Alex Manske.

While Manske has been away from the team recovering from an injury, Christopher Newport transfer Connor Barry has made a name for himself, drawing a lot of praise from Campbell.

“Connor Barry, he’s come in, and he’s really done a great job, probably been the surprise so far, of four days of practice, just his ability to get quality reps,” Campbell said on March 31.

However, Barry’s Penn State debut at Blue-White Practice warranted mixed reactions. The senior quarterback threw a passing touchdown and rushed for a score, but also threw an interception.

Despite the underwhelming performance, Barry has put himself in a position to battle Manske for the No. 2 spot, a contest that will go deep into summer.

Outside of Becht, Manske and Barry, none of the other three quarterbacks had great showings at the spring practice. Jack Lambert has yet to see any playing time despite three years in the program, while freshmen Kase Evans and Peyton Falzone showed youthful struggles.

Who will cement themselves on the defensive line?

Penn State’s defensive end and defensive tackle rooms boast very different experience levels. The defensive end room has mostly younger players, while veteran players highlight the defensive tackles.

Despite this, both starting positions are still up in the air as players recover from injury and others continue to show their potential at practice.

Yvan Kemajou and Max Granville — both returners from last year’s squad — have emerged as two potential starters at defensive end. Kemajou burned his redshirt last season and was one of the top freshmen on the team, while Granville missed the entirety of 2025 with an injury.

However, Colorado transfer Alex McPherson and Iowa State transfer Ike Ezeogu, who started 11 games last year, are also in the mix.

At defensive tackle, defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe brought a pair of veterans in Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams with him from UCLA, while Oklahoma State transfer Armstrong Nnodim and Utah transfer Dallas Vakalahi have also been names to watch.

Despite being the youngest of the bunch as just a sophomore, Nnodim has drawn praise from Campbell and other players on the team as a strong competitor throughout spring practice.

Will Penn State be fully healthy Week 1?

The Nittany Lions were missing several pieces of their potential starting lineup this spring due to ongoing injuries. Becht was limited along with wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

Meanwhile, Granville, tight end Andrew Rappleyea, starting offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh, center Dom Rulli and linebackers Tony Rojas and Alex Tatsch are expected to be at full strength at the start of the season, barring any setbacks.

Penn State has struggled at wide receiver in recent years, and with Sowell and Eskildsen recovering during spring, fans have yet to see if those struggles will continue again with a different starting squad.

Along the offensive line, Anthony Donkoh missed spring practice following an unspecified medical procedure. He’s expected to hit the starting lineups when he returns, likely for the start of the season.

On defense, Rojas is one of the biggest names who’s yet to see the field this spring, as he tore his ACL during practice last season ahead of Penn State’s game against UCLA.

When he returns to the field, Rojas will likely start for the third straight season. He’s on track to return for Week 1, but was sidelined during all of spring ball.

During Rojas’ absence in the fall, then-freshman Alex Tatsch stepped up and played in six games. However, he too missed spring practice to injury, and will look for a role in the linebacker room when healthy.

Granville is another big name who’s looking to grace the starting lineup. He’s yet to establish himself as a top defender on Penn State’s defense, but with Dani Dennis-Sutton, Zuriah Fisher and Chaz Coleman gone, he has the opportunity to step up and be a starter if he’s healthy.

title: Penn State Athletics announces 2026 football student ticket information
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State Athletics has announced the initial information for student football tickets for the 2026 season.

The system will run as a lottery, with the seven-game ticket package totaling $246, the same price and system as the past two years.

The ticket window opens on July 20, with the graduate students' request window. Those students will have until Monday, July 27, to request tickets.

The senior ticket window opens at 9 a.m. on Tuesday, July 21, and closes at 5 p.m. on July 28, giving each class a week to submit for tickets. Juniors can begin requesting tickets at 9 a.m. on July 22 and close one week later at 5 p.m. on July 29.

The sophomore class requests next, beginning at 9 a.m. on July 23, closing at 5 p.m. on July 30, with the freshmen rounding out the request window at 9 a.m. on July 24, with it closing at 5 p.m. on July 31.

Students will be notified during the week of Aug. 3 via email if their request was selected.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse set for NCAA Tournament matchup against Stanford
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Three hundred and fifty two days after being named the 10th head coach in Penn State history, Kayla Treanor and the Nittany Lions will compete in the NCAA tournament.

Penn State will face Stanford at 6 p.m. on Friday in the first round of the tournament, marking its first appearance since 2024.

While the two teams have only faced off twice in program history, the Nittany Lions will look to earn their first NCAA tournament victory since 2017 on Friday.

All-time matchups

The two squads faced off in a home-and-home series in 2015 and 2016, each claiming a victory.

The Nittany Lions opened with a 17-15 victory in 2015, upsetting No. 9 Stanford. Penn State went on a 10-3 run starting with three minutes left to ultimately win the game with four hat tricks.

Things went a little differently the second time around, as No. 8 Penn State fell 16-10 to the Cardinal on the road, being upset in enemy territory.

While the blue and white never led, an 8-1 run spanning between the second and third quarter put the nail in the coffin as Penn State fell by six goals to even the series.

Penn State’s tournament history

Penn State has made the NCAA tournament 27 times in program history, advancing past its first game just 13 times.

The Nittany Lions have struggled in recent tournament appearances, falling in the first round three times in the past eight years. Outside of those faults, Penn State was last successful in 2017, when it earned a spot in the Final Four for the second year in a row.

While the blue and white has yet to make it to the big game in the 21st century, it had lots of success from joining the NCAA in 1983 to the late 1990s.

Penn State earned nine Final Four appearances in 16 years, emerging victorious two times.

The Nittany Lions won NCAA championships in 1987 and 1989, defeating Temple and Harvard, respectively.

2026 Cardinal

Stanford had a dominant start to the season, winning 10 straight games.

Through the first two months of action, the Cardinal earned five top-25 victories, including wins against No. 9 Clemson, No. 13 Syracuse, No. 21 Virginia, No. 22 Duke and No. 11 Notre Dame.

After falling to Boston College and getting upset by Villanova, Stanford bounced back with a 17-goal victory against Louisville before losing its third game of the season to North Carolina, which it also lost to in the season finale and ACC title game.

The Cardinal have put up the fifth-most goals of any team in the nation this year, scoring 272 goals through 19 contests.

However, no player emerged as the frontrunner in scoring this season. Junior Ava Arceri leads the team with 46 goals, followed by redshirt junior Aliya Polisky with 44 goals.

In addition to the high-scoring offense, Stanford has been led by a pair of dominant goalkeepers in Lucy Pearson and Ella Morton, who have shared the net this year.

Pearson started the first nine games of the season before missing seven games due to injury. She returned for the last three games of the year, posting a 43.4% save rate on the season. In her absence, Morton posted a 43.8% save record in her seven starts.

Friday’s matchup

It will be over two weeks between Penn State’s Big Ten quarterfinal game against Michigan and the NCAA first round contest on Friday, giving plenty of time for rest for Penn State.

The Nittany Lions are coming off a successful first regular season for Treanor, winning 12 games — three of which were against top-25 opponents.

Outside of freshman phenom Rocquette Allen, who was named Big Ten Freshman of the Year last week, Penn State sought production from Kelly MacKinney and Payton Wainman, who all had over 40 goals on the year.

Penn State and Stanford rank as the 15th and 16th-highest scoring offenses in the country, averaging 14.56 and 14.32 points per game.

Friday’s game will be a high-scoring affair, but will likely be largely affected by draw controls, an area where Penn State’s struggled this year. The Nittany Lions have won just 47.9% of their draw controls, while Stanford has won 52.2%.

The Cardinal are coming off a first round victory in last year’s tournament and will look to carry that into this year’s performance, while Penn State looks for its first victory since 2017.
